introSection: Spiral was a steel roller coaster built by American manufacturer Arrow Development that was previously located at Formosa Fun Coast in New Taipei City, Taiwan.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
The ride originally opened as Screamroller in Worlds of Fun on April 10, 1976. On May 31, 1983, the ride changed its name to Extremeroller (or EXT, depending on how the sign in front was interpreted) and received stand-up trains, making it the first Stand-up coaster with inversions in North America. The trains were already recalled at the end of the season, which was common for Arrow Stand-up trains, but the ride's name wasn't converted back into Screamroller.

The ride closed in 1988 to make way for Timber Wolf, and it was sold to Formosa Fun Coast in Taiwan.

When the ride closed in Formosa Fun Coast, it was put in storage on the park's parking lot for 10 years before being scrapped.

sections.title: Trains
sections.content: The ride originally used trains built by Arrow Development. The trains were replaced with Stand-up ones in 1983, these only lasted for one year, before the ride reverted back to its original trains. ACE has the old Arrow stand-up trains in storage.

sections.title: Incidents
sections.content: In June 1976, an 8-year-old was struck by the ride while standing in a fenced-in restricted area. He received major head injuries. A jury awarded the boy $1.4 million in December 1977, finding the park owners solely responsible for the accident.

fullText:
Spiral was a steel roller coaster built by American manufacturer Arrow Development that was previously located at Formosa Fun Coast in New Taipei City, Taiwan.

The ride originally opened as Screamroller in Worlds of Fun on April 10, 1976. On May 31, 1983, the ride changed its name to Extremeroller (or EXT, depending on how the sign in front was interpreted) and received stand-up trains, making it the first Stand-up coaster with inversions in North America. The trains were already recalled at the end of the season, which was common for Arrow Stand-up trains, but the ride's name wasn't converted back into Screamroller.

The ride closed in 1988 to make way for Timber Wolf, and it was sold to Formosa Fun Coast in Taiwan.

When the ride closed in Formosa Fun Coast, it was put in storage on the park's parking lot for 10 years before being scrapped.



The ride originally used trains built by Arrow Development. The trains were replaced with Stand-up ones in 1983, these only lasted for one year, before the ride reverted back to its original trains. ACE has the old Arrow stand-up trains in storage.

In June 1976, an 8-year-old was struck by the ride while standing in a fenced-in restricted area. He received major head injuries. A jury awarded the boy $1.4 million in December 1977, finding the park owners solely responsible for the accident.
